past_medical_history: Aortic stenosis (moderate), diagnosed 3 years ago on echocardiogram
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago, on insulin and oral agents
Chronic kidney disease stage 3b (baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL)
family_history: Father died suddenly at age 58, cause unknown, presumed cardiac
Mother had hypertension and stroke, died at age 81
One brother with a pacemaker for 'slow heart rhythm,' placed at age 70

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 25-pack-year history, quit 18 years ago
social_history.alcohol: One to two beers on weekends, denies heavy drinking
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his wife in a two-story home; bedroom is on the second floor
social_history.exercise: Used to walk half a mile daily but has stopped over the past two months due to increasing fatigue and lightheadedness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate, volume, and prosody. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, able to perform serial sevens correctly. Recalls 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion or post-ictal features at time of examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. No relative afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopic exam shows mild arteriolar narrowing bilaterally consistent with chronic hypertension, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus or diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all three divisions bilaterally. Jaw strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial droop. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline, gag reflex present. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline, no atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal, no rigidity, spasticity, or cogwheeling. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups tested bilaterally including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, hip flexors, quadriceps, hamstrings, ankle dorsiflexors, and plantarflexors. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout upper and lower extremities. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Vibration mildly reduced at bilateral great toes and medial malleoli, consistent with mild length-dependent peripheral neuropathy.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal stride length and arm swing. Slightly wide base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or evidence of oral trauma. No urinary incontinence noted. Right hip tenderness from fall but no deformity. No bruising pattern suggestive of prolonged convulsive activity.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden collapse while walking up the stairs at home
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Harold Simmons is a 74-year-old man with a history of moderate aortic stenosis, hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and chronic kidney disease who was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after a witnessed sudden collapse. According to his wife, the patient was walking up the stairs to the second floor of their home at approximately 6:45 PM when he suddenly dropped to the ground on the landing without any warning. She was walking behind him and saw his body go limp as he fell forward, landing on his right side. She reports that he appeared very pale and his lips looked bluish for several seconds. She did not observe any jerking, shaking, stiffening, or rhythmic limb movements during the event. He was unresponsive for approximately 15 to 20 seconds, and then his eyes opened and he began speaking immediately, asking his wife why she was screaming and what had happened. He had no confusion, disorientation, or drowsiness after the event, and he was able to describe exactly what he had been doing before he blacked out. There was no tongue biting, no urinary or fecal incontinence, and no witnessed seizure-like activity. The patient himself recalls feeling completely well one moment and then suddenly waking up on the floor the next, with no preceding lightheadedness, nausea, chest pain, palpitations, or visual changes. Over the past six to eight weeks, he reports three prior episodes of near-syncope where his vision would suddenly go gray and he would feel as if he were about to pass out, each lasting a few seconds. Two of these occurred while climbing stairs and one while pushing a shopping cart at the grocery store. He has also noticed progressive exertional fatigue and dyspnea on exertion that has worsened over the past two to three months, to the point where he can no longer walk more than a block without needing to rest. He has a known cardiac murmur that he was told was getting worse at his last cardiology visit six months ago, but he missed his follow-up appointment. He denies fever, recent illness, chest pain at rest, orthopnea, or lower extremity edema.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and well-modulated, reactive to eye opening and closure
background.sleep_features: Stage II sleep captured with vertex waves and sleep spindles, no epileptiform abnormalities in sleep

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not significantly obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric buildup of generalized slow activity that resolved within 1 minute of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response at 12-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG. No epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording. The clinical episode is unlikely to be epileptic in nature based on these findings. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording; a single normal interictal EEG does not definitively exclude epilepsy, but taken in clinical context, a non-epileptic etiology is favored.

ecg.rhythm: Complete heart block (third-degree AV block) with wide-complex ventricular escape rhythm

intervals.QRS: 156 ms (widened, left bundle branch block morphology in escape beats)

findings: Complete AV dissociation with independent atrial rate of approximately 82 bpm (P waves marching through QRS complexes)
Ventricular escape rhythm at 32 bpm with wide QRS and LBBB morphology
Prolonged QTc interval at 512 ms, increasing risk of polymorphic ventricular tachycardia
No acute ST elevation to suggest acute myocardial infarction
Diffuse non-specific ST-T wave changes
Low-voltage QRS in limb leads
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria in precordial leads
ecg.interpretation: Third-degree (complete) atrioventricular block with unreliable wide-complex ventricular escape rhythm at 32 beats per minute. Complete AV dissociation confirmed. Widened QRS with LBBB morphology. This is a high-risk ECG requiring immediate cardiology consultation, continuous monitoring, and preparation for transcutaneous or transvenous pacing with urgent evaluation for permanent pacemaker implantation.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 35.8 % (L), MCV 78.7 fL (L), Sodium 146 mEq/L (H), Potassium 5.3 mEq/L (H), BUN 38 mg/dL (H), Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (H), Glucose 164 mg/dL (H), Troponin I (high-sensitivity) 0.06 ng/mL (H), BNP 614 pg/mL (H), Phosphorus 4.7 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 7.8 % (H).

Echocardiogram.value: Heavily calcified aortic annulus with extension into the intervalvular fibrosa

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left ventricular ejection fraction 40% % (H), Left ventricular end-diastolic diameter 5.7 cm (H), Interventricular septum thickness 1.4 cm (H), Posterior wall thickness 1.3 cm (H), Aortic valve area 0.9 cm2 (H), Aortic valve mean gradient 42 mmHg (H), Aortic valve peak velocity 4.3 m/s (H), Left atrial diameter 4.5 cm (H), Mitral valve Mild to moderate regurgitation qualitative (H), Aortic root Heavily calcified aortic annulus with extension into the intervalvular fibrosa qualitative (H), Right ventricular function Mildly reduced qualitative (H), Estimated RVSP 48 mmHg (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Mitral valve: Mild to moderate regurgitation qualitative (H)
Aortic root: Heavily calcified aortic annulus with extension into the intervalvular fibrosa qualitative (H)
Right ventricular function: Mildly reduced qualitative (L)

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block with wide-complex ventricular escape rhythm throughout 24-hour recording
findings: Persistent complete AV block throughout the entire 24-hour recording period
Average ventricular rate 34 bpm (range 26-40 bpm)
Atrial rate averaging 80 bpm, predominantly sinus rhythm
Minimum ventricular rate 26 bpm at 02:18 AM with 5.8-second RR pause
Maximum ventricular rate 40 bpm during brief wheelchair transport
Three short runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (3-5 beats), rate 140-160 bpm
Frequent PVCs (186 total in 24 hours, 0.5% of total beats)
QTc exceeded 500 ms during multiple overnight intervals
output.interpretation: Critically slow rates overnight with pauses up to 5.8 seconds represent periods of high risk for asystolic cardiac arrest. Non-sustained ventricular tachycardia in the setting of prolonged QTc raises the risk for torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death. This is a life-threatening conduction disturbance requiring urgent permanent pacemaker implantation.

Tilt_table_test.value: Severe lightheadedness and graying of vision at 2 minutes, near-syncope at 3 minutes, test aborted

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Baseline supine HR 33 bpm (H), 70-degree tilt BP at 1 min 88/48 mmHg (H), 70-degree tilt HR at 1 min 34 bpm (H), 70-degree tilt BP at 3 min 78/42 mmHg (H), Symptoms during tilt Severe lightheadedness and graying of vision at 2 minutes, near-syncope at 3 minutes, test aborted qualitative (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Symptoms during tilt: Severe lightheadedness and graying of vision at 2 minutes, near-syncope at 3 minutes, test aborted qualitative (L)

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block; contraindicated in current clinical state
findings: Exercise stress test was NOT performed due to absolute contraindications
Patient has severe symptomatic aortic stenosis (AVA 0.9 cm2) — absolute contraindication to exercise stress testing
Complete heart block with hemodynamically significant bradycardia — additional relative contraindication
Prolonged QTc >500 ms with non-sustained VT on Holter — additional concern
Resting ECG obtained instead confirms persistent complete heart block at 32 bpm
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test is absolutely contraindicated in this patient due to severe symptomatic aortic stenosis. Exercising a patient with severe aortic stenosis and complete heart block carries a high risk of syncope, ventricular arrhythmia, and sudden death. No stress testing should be performed until both the conduction abnormality and valvular disease have been addressed.

findings.type: Severe aortic valve calcification with annular extension
findings.location: Aortic valve, aortic annulus, and intervalvular fibrosa extending toward the membranous septum
findings.size: Extensive calcification measuring approximately 2.2 cm in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T1: Signal void consistent with dense calcification at the aortic annulus
signal_characteristics.cine: Restricted aortic valve leaflet motion with severe stenosis, turbulent jet
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Patchy mid-wall enhancement in the basal interventricular septum adjacent to the aortic annular calcification, consistent with reactive fibrosis at the conduction system level

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly elevated native T1 values diffusely (1120 ms, normal <1050 ms), suggesting diffuse interstitial fibrosis
signal_characteristics.ECV: Elevated extracellular volume fraction at 32% (normal <28%), consistent with diffuse myocardial fibrosis

volumetrics.LV_mass: 178 g (significantly increased, consistent with severe LVH)

additional_observations: Mild right ventricular dilation with mildly reduced systolic function
No evidence of amyloid infiltration pattern (no diffuse subendocardial or transmural enhancement)
No evidence of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (no asymmetric hypertrophy, no SAM)
output.impression: This calcific infiltration of the conduction system provides a clear structural explanation for the patient's complete heart block. LVEF is reduced at 40%. No evidence of infiltrative cardiomyopathy, sarcoidosis, or ARVC. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT_Head_Report.value: Normal size and configuration, no hydrocephalus
Moderate periventricular and subcortical white matter hypodensities
No fracture, small right temporal scalp hematoma

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Calvarium and soft tissues No fracture, small right temporal scalp hematoma qualitative (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Calvarium and soft tissues: No fracture, small right temporal scalp hematoma qualitative (H)

findings.location: Periventricular and deep white matter, bilateral

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense confluent periventricular and scattered deep white matter lesions

additional_observations: No enhancing lesions on post-gadolinium sequences
Mild generalized cortical volume loss appropriate for age
Normal major intracranial arterial flow voids
output.impression: No acute infarction, hemorrhage, mass, or demyelinating disease. The brain MRI does not identify a neurological cause for the syncopal episode. The cardiac etiology remains the primary consideration. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block secondary to calcific aortic stenosis with conduction system involvement

differential.key_distinguishing: No witnessed tonic-clonic activity, no post-ictal confusion, immediate return to full alertness, no tongue biting or incontinence, normal EEG.
No identifiable vasovagal trigger, no prodromal nausea or diaphoresis, exertional pattern more consistent with cardiac etiology, age and cardiac risk factors argue against vasovagal.

Syncope occurred during exertion (climbing stairs) not on standing from sitting or lying. Patient is on tamsulosin and amlodipine which can cause orthostatic hypotension, but any orthostatic component is secondary to fixed bradycardia.

Severe aortic stenosis can independently cause exertional syncope through inability to augment cardiac output; however, the complete heart block provides the primary mechanism and is the more immediately actionable diagnosis.
No focal neurological symptoms, TIA very rarely causes true loss of consciousness, normal neurological exam, normal brain imaging.

optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complete heart block with ventricular escape rhythm at 32 bpm, prolonged QTc 512 ms

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous telemetry monitoring and establish IV access
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Persistent bradycardia with complete AV dissociation, monitor for pauses and ventricular arrhythmia

optimal_actions.action: Apply transcutaneous pacing pads and have atropine and isoproterenol available at bedside
Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, coagulation studies, TSH, magnesium
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No metabolic cause for syncope; mildly elevated troponin (demand), significantly elevated BNP

optimal_actions.action: Urgent cardiology consultation for temporary pacing and permanent pacemaker planning
Obtain EEG to rule out epileptiform etiology for loss of consciousness
Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate structural heart disease and aortic stenosis severity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe aortic stenosis (AVA 0.9 cm2), reduced LVEF 40%, LVH, calcification extending toward conduction system

optimal_actions.action: Review and adjust medications: hold tamsulosin (alpha-blocker causing vasodilation), continue losartan with caution given potassium
Admit to CCU or monitored telemetry bed for continuous monitoring pending pacemaker implantation
Plan for permanent pacemaker implantation, with consideration for CRT-P given reduced LVEF, and subsequent evaluation for TAVR for severe symptomatic aortic stenosis

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to identify complete heart block
Apply transcutaneous pacing pads as a safety measure given critically slow rate and prolonged QTc
Urgent cardiology consultation for temporary and permanent pacing
Recognize that syncope is cardiac in origin (not epileptic) despite the dramatic nature of the collapse
Identify severe aortic stenosis as the underlying etiology of the conduction disease
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs without evidence of epilepsy
Administering calcium channel blockers (amlodipine should be held, and non-dihydropyridine CCBs like verapamil or diltiazem are absolutely contraindicated as they suppress AV conduction)
Performing exercise stress test in a patient with severe symptomatic aortic stenosis (absolute contraindication)
Discharging the patient without cardiac monitoring and pacemaker evaluation
Administering IV adenosine (would worsen AV block)
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication and would delay critical cardiac management)
key_reasoning_points: Sudden unheralded loss of consciousness during exertion with no prodrome and immediate return to full alertness is the hallmark of cardiac syncope, not epilepsy
The absence of tonic-clonic movements, post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, and incontinence argues strongly against seizure
Exertional pattern of syncope and pre-syncopal episodes raises concern for both cardiac arrhythmia and severe aortic stenosis, both of which are confirmed
The ECG showing complete heart block with a ventricular escape rate of 32 bpm and prolonged QTc provides the immediate diagnosis and explains the syncopal mechanism
Severe aortic stenosis with calcification extending into the conduction system is the underlying structural etiology of the complete heart block
The significantly elevated BNP (642 pg/mL) indicates substantial cardiac dysfunction from the combined hemodynamic burden of aortic stenosis and bradycardia
The normal EEG confirms a non-epileptic etiology for the transient loss of consciousness
This patient requires a dual management strategy: urgent pacemaker implantation for the conduction disease, followed by evaluation for TAVR for the severe aortic stenosis

clinical_pearls: Severe calcific aortic stenosis is a well-recognized cause of complete heart block due to extension of calcification from the aortic annulus into the adjacent conduction system (His bundle, proximal bundle branches)
Exertional syncope in the setting of aortic stenosis is a red flag for severe disease and carries prognostic significance indicating the need for valve intervention
Complete heart block with a wide-complex escape rhythm indicates an infranodal block, which is less likely to respond to atropine and more likely to require pacing
Prolonged QTc (>500 ms) in the setting of severe bradycardia significantly increases the risk of torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death
Exercise stress testing is absolutely contraindicated in severe symptomatic aortic stenosis
